input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Digital Twin is built around a simple thermal incubator – an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heat‑bed, a fan, two temperature sensors inside the box and one sensor outside. The box is placed on a Raspberry Pi‑controlled enclosure that monitors the internal and ambient temperatures and steers the heat‑bed and fan to maintain a desired set‑point temperature irrespective of the box contents. This simple yet canonical CPS has a well‑defined cyber (control logic on Raspberry Pi plus communication stack), a physical plant (insulated container, heat‑bed, fan and sensors) and an environment (ambient temperature) that together make it a clean platform for proving digital‑twin concepts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: 1. Real‑time visualization: ingestion of controller and driver messages into a time‑series database and display on a dashboard. 2. On‑demand calibration: automated execution of calibration routines described in section 3.3, analogous to tracking simulators. 3. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation: Kalman‑filter based inference of the non‑measured heat‑bed temperature, flagging anomalies. 4. What‑if simulations: co‑simulation using historical or live PT data to evaluate controller parameter settings. 5. Self‑adaptation loop: upon detecting altered plant behaviour, the DT schedules adaptive experiments, recalibrates Model B, reconfigures the Kalman filter and controller to maintain optimal operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on an open‑source repository (github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example-incubator) that provides hardware and software specifications. The physical layer uses a Raspberry Pi with connected heatbed, fan and temperature sensors. Data acquisition is performed by Python software that interfaces with the sensors and publishes over a lightweight communication middleware. Digital Twin models (two‑parameter Model A and four‑parameter Model B) are executed within the same Python environment, and calibration uses data‑fitting to update model parameters. States are estimated with a Kalman filter algorithm. Real‑time data are stored in a time‑series database and visualised via a dashboard. Co‑simulation of model dynamics with live data is employed for what‑if scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin contains a set of computational models that represent the thermal dynamics of the insulated box and its heatbed. Two differential‑equation models have been developed: Model A contains two free parameters and Model B contains four free parameters, with the latter capturing the thermal inertia of the heatbed more accurately. The models are calibrated against in‑field data by a non‑linear least‑squares fitting routine that minimizes the squared error between simulated temperature trajectories and the time series recorded by the DS18S20 sensors. Data for the Twin are collected continuously from the physical twin through the Raspberry Pi: two DS18S20 temperature sensors inside the box and one DSS18S20 sensor outside. The sensor readings are retrieved over a single 1‑Wire bus, processed by the Pi, and forwarded to the Twin core. Within the Twin, the calibrated model parameters are stored and used for several purposes: a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature and flags anomalous plant behavior; run‑time state prediction used in real‑time monitoring dashboards; and co‑simulation runs that execute “what‑if” scenario explorations to refine controller settings. The Twin internally represents the data and models as paired entities, where each model is linked to its corresponding measurement set, allowing reproducible simulation, calibration, and anomaly detection workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator is performed by a heat‑bed heater and an internal fan. Both actuators are driven from the Raspberry Pi through GPIO outputs whose control signals are generated by the controller’s temperature regulation loop. Remote interaction is achieved via the controller’s network interface, allowing the Digital Twin to modify set‑points and actuator schedules during self‑adaptation. Safety limits are enforced by the controller, interrupting actuation when the internal temperature deviates beyond defined safe thresholds.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is performed by three DS18S20 High‑Precision 1‑Wire Digital Thermometers: two are mounted on the interior of the styrofoam box to capture the air temperature where the embryos reside, and a single sensor is positioned on the external side of the box to monitor ambient temperature. The sensors communicate with the Raspberry Pi over a single 1‑Wire bus, allowing multiple devices to share the same data line. The Pi polls the sensors at a looped interval of 1 second, reading the 12‑bit temperature value reported by each DS18S20. The sensor readings are packaged into JSON messages and forwarded to the Twin’s data ingestion pipeline over the local network.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a two‑step process: first, each model is calibrated using a non‑linear least‑squares solver against a representative set of experimental data; second, the calibrated model is validated by comparing its predictions to an independent measurement trajectory, computing the root‑mean‑square error as the quantitative metric. Uncertainty in the models originates from variability in the heatbed’s thermal inertia and from random sensor noise; these are propagated through a Kalman filter that not only estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature but also provides real‑time residuals that serve as anomaly indicators. When residuals exceed a statistically-defined threshold, the Twin’s monitor triggers a safety shutdown or logs the violation for further review. The entire calibration and validation workflow is automated and can be re‑run on demand (e.g., when a new load is introduced) to maintain fidelity. Additionally, co‑simulations that replay historical or live data are used to perform “what‑if” analyses, providing an operational validation of controller settings and further reinforcing the quality assurance process.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The incubator’s physical twin records temperature using two sensors inside the styrofoam box and a third sensor outside the box. A low‑level driver running on a Raspberry Pi periodically samples these sensors and publishes the raw temperature values to a RabbitMQ communication server. The DT subscribes to the corresponding RabbitMQ topics to ingest the data for monitoring, simulation, and model‑calibration tasks. The documents do not specify the sampling interval, message payload format, or any preprocessing or data‑quality checks performed prior to ingestion.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the DT are transmitted over RabbitMQ to the Raspberry Pi controller, which interprets them as actuation requests for the fan and the heatbed. The controller operates as a bang‑bang regulator, using the parameters lower limit (LL), upper limit (UL), heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C) to decide when to turn the heater or fan on or off. The low‑level driver receives these commands from the controller via the communication server and actuates the hardware. The controller reports its state back to RabbitMQ, providing feedback of the executed command. The documents provide no details on command authentication, conflict resolution, or explicit confirmation protocols beyond this state reporting.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All data and control flow between the physical and virtual twins is mediated by a RabbitMQ message broker. The broker is deployed as part of the software stack and provides an AMQP‑based messaging layer that connects the low‑level driver, the Raspberry Pi controller, and the DT services. The documents do not describe network topology beyond this single‑broker architecture, nor do they detail bandwidth, latency, or redundancy requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The core software components – the low‑level driver, the controller executing a bang‑bang logic, and the RabbitMQ communication server – are all hosted on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. This edge deployment is supported by the open‑source repository released by the authors. The documents do not provide explicit computational or storage resource specifications, nor do they describe additional deployment models, scalability mechanisms, or availability guarantees beyond the single‑device setup.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Using the incubator case study, decision support is enabled through real‑time monitoring of temperature data streams, fault detection, and anomaly detection via a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature. The filter fuses data from the two internal PT sensors and one external temperature sensor and runs continuously to produce state estimates and covariance‐based confidence measures. The estimated data are stored in a time‑series database and visualised on a dashboard, providing on‑demand calibration and heat‑bed state estimation. What‑if analysis is performed by running co‑simulations that feed historical or real‑time physical‑twin measurements into Model B, enabling optimisation of controller parameters. These combined capabilities let the Digital Twin generate actionable insights on safety, efficiency, and controller tuning with measurable confidence.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The digital twin framework for the incubator incorporates real‑time safety monitoring by running dedicated monitor algorithms that continuously evaluate the physical twin’s operating state against safety and optimality thresholds. Calibration algorithms update the embedded physics models using incoming sensor data (two temperature sensors inside the styrofoam box and one outside), ensuring the model predictions stay synchronized with the measured temperature dynamics. When a monitor detects a violation—such as a deviation from the target temperature band or anomalous heat‑bed performance—the system generates an alert that can be handled by a human operator or an automated safety‑action module. This integration of monitoring and calibration establishes a safety‑critical feedback loop that maintains the incubator within safe operating limits while enabling adaptive control of the heater and fan. No specific cybersecurity or access‑control measures are described in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate, detailed, and directly grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies the system as a thermal incubator CPS, lists all specific components (styrofoam box, heat-bed, fan, temperature sensors, Raspberry Pi), and describes its purpose and architecture (cyber, physical, environment). Supported by quotes: 'simple yet representative thermal incubator system', 'The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed', 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller', 'Two temperature sensors... One temperature sensor', 'regulate it regardless of the content inside'.

The extracted characteristic lists five services, but the provided source text does not explicitly detail all of them. 'Real-time visualization' and 'On-demand calibration' are partially supported; the abstract mentions 'monitoring', and Section 3.3 describes 'Calibration' using a 'non-linear least squares solver'. However, 'Anomaly detection and heat-bed state estimation' involving a 'Kalman-filter' is not mentioned in the snippets; the text discusses simulation models (Model A and Model B) but not Kalman filters for anomaly detection. 'What-if simulations' is supported by the abstract's mention of 'run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT'. 'Self-adaptation loop' involving reconfiguring the controller is not found in the provided text; Section 5 is referenced in the introduction as future work but its content is absent. Thus, the extraction includes unsupported technical details (Kalman filter, self-adaptation loop) leading to a lower score.

The extraction lists specific tools that are mostly supported by the text. The 'open-source repository' is mentioned: 'hardware and software used in this case study are available open source' with a GitHub link. 'Raspberry Pi' is confirmed in Section 2.1.4. 'Python software' is implied in Section 3.3 regarding the 'python Scipy package'. 'RabbitMQ' is confirmed in Section 2.2.1 as the communication server. 'Model A' and 'Model B' are detailed in Section 3. However, 'time-series database' and 'dashboard' are not mentioned in the provided snippets, though the abstract mentions 'monitoring'. 'Co-simulation of model dynamics with live data' is not explicitly described; the text mentions 'simulations' but not co-simulation with live data. Despite these omissions, the core tools (Raspberry Pi, RabbitMQ, Python, Models) are accurately described and specific.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions that a RabbitMQ server is used for 'synchronisation and communication' and that the driver 'periodically reads the temperature data'. However, it does not specify the synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or specific time scales for different services as requested by the characteristic definition. Therefore, the claim 'Not in Document' is justified.

The document describes a single incubator system. While it mentions two simulation models (Model A and Model B), these are alternative physical models used for calibration, not multiple digital twin instances or a decentralized hierarchy. No multiplicities or coordination between twins are described.

While the source describes the architecture of the specific incubator's digital twin components ('controller', 'low-level driver', 'communication server'), it does not describe a 'constellation' involving the orchestration of multiple twins or a complex federated system.

The source document focuses on the internal architecture of the incubator case study, detailing the communication between the controller, driver, and sensors via RabbitMQ. It does not discuss integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other enterprise systems, or other digital twins.

the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides an excellent summary of the two simulation models (Model A and B), their parameter counts, and the thermal inertia focus, which are all explicitly in the text. It also correctly describes the calibration method (non-linear least squares) and the sensor types used. However, it speculates on the implementation of a 'Kalman filter' for state estimation within the Twin, which is not mentioned as the specific method used in this case study (it appears only as a general example in the DT definition section).

Accurately describes the physical actuators (heat-bed and fan) and their control via Raspberry Pi GPIO, supported by the text: 'The plant of the Incubator consists of... A heatbed... A fan' and 'GPIO connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect... fan, and the heatbed'. The description of the control loop and the potential for remote interaction via the network interface is well-grounded in the software setup description, which mentions 'RabbitMQ server... for delivering data' and the controller listening for commands.

The extraction correctly identifies the sensor model (DS18S20), the quantity (three), placement (two inside, one outside), and communication bus (1-Wire). However, it adds specific technical details not found in the text, such as the polling interval (1 second), the resolution (12-bit), and the data packaging format (JSON).

The extraction correctly describes the calibration procedure using a non-linear least squares solver to minimize residual error. However, it heavily relies on general definitions of Digital Twins (from Section 1) to describe the system's fidelity management, specifically claiming the use of a Kalman filter and specific anomaly detection workflows, which are not detailed as part of the implemented Incubator system in the provided text.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This

lated research areas.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus

To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative

inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.


4 [https://www.rabbitmq.com/](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)


7


Figure 6: Schematic of the software deployment of the components.


The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:


8


|Physical Twin|Digital Twin|
|---|---|
|||









Figure 7: Overview of communication in the Incubator. The arrows represent the information flow.


**LL** – Lower limit for temperature;
**UL** – Upper limit for temperature;
**H** – Heating duration;
**C** – Waiting duration.















Figure 8: Controller Statechart.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.

Supported by quotes: 'Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box', 'The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data... uploaded to the communication server', and 'DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed description of the control logic, correctly identifying the bang-bang controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C) and the flow of commands via RabbitMQ to the driver. While the specific origin of commands to the controller (DT) is implied rather than explicitly stated, the technical details of the controller's operation are well-supported. C – Waiting duration', 'The low level driver... checks if there are commands from the controller', and 'sharing the control states to the RabbitMQ server'.

It accurately notes the lack of details regarding network topology, bandwidth, and redundancy. However, it mentions 'AMQP-based messaging layer', which is not found in the provided source text (the text only mentions 'RabbitMQ'). Supported by quotes: 'Rabbit MQ server... for synchronisation and communication', 'running on the raspberry pi itself', and 'DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages'.

The extraction precisely identifies the hosting platform (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) for the controller and communication server, and correctly infers the driver's deployment based on the hardware context. It also correctly notes the open-source nature of the repository and the lack of detailed resource specifications. Supported by quotes: 'Rabbit MQ server, running on the raspberry pi itself', 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B... was selected to implement the controller', and 'hardware and software used in this case study are available open source'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with
